perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, without reference to conflict of laws principles. The exclusive venue for any dispute relating to this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts within Santa Clara County, California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California, but only to the extent that they align with Google's business interests, without reference to conflict of laws principles. The exclusive venue for any dispute relating to this Agreement shall be in the state or federal courts within Santa Clara County, California.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the choice of law clause to prioritize Google's business interests creates uncertainty and potential unenforceability. While parties can generally agree on a choice of law, such a clause is void if it violates public policy or fundamental principles of fairness. Saying the governing law only applies if it benefits one party introduces a conflict.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Civil Code § 1668 - Contracts Contrary to Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts California Civil Code § 1668, which prohibits contracts that are contrary to public policy. A clause that dictates the applicability of California law only when it aligns with one party's business interests could be deemed against public policy as it undermines the principle of impartial application of the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition that California law only applies if it aligns with Google's business interests. This directly contradicts the principle of impartial application of the law. California Civil Code § 1668 states that "All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law." The change in the contract would allow Google to potentially avoid legal obligations if those obligations conflict with its business interests, which falls under contracts against public policy, as described by the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: In connection with Participant's visit, Google may disclose or expose to Participant certain information that is proprietary to Google ("Confidential Information"). Participant will use the Confidential Information only for the purpose associated with Participant's visit. Participant will use the same degree of care, but no less than a reasonable degree of care, as Participant uses with respect to its own similar information to protect the Confidential Information and to prevent (i) any use of Confidential Information not authorized in this Agreement, (ii) dissemination of Confidential Information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: In connection with Participant's visit, Google may disclose or expose to Participant certain information that is proprietary to Google ("Confidential Information"). Participant will use the Confidential Information only for the purpose associated with Participant's visit. Participant will use the degree of care they deem appropriate, with no obligation to prevent unauthorized use or dissemination.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a 'reasonable degree of care.' Replacing this with 'the degree of care they deem appropriate' removes any objective standard for protecting confidential information. Eliminating the obligation to prevent unauthorized use or dissemination directly contradicts the purpose of a non-disclosure agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in California)
perturbation.law_explanation: This change contradicts the spirit and intent of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (as adopted in California), specifically Cal. Civ. Code § 3426.1, which provides remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By removing the requirement to prevent unauthorized use or dissemination, the modified text effectively nullifies the protection afforded to trade secrets under the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the protection of confidential information, contradicting the spirit and intent of trade secret laws. The original text mandates a 'reasonable degree of care' and prevents unauthorized use or dissemination, aligning with the duty to maintain secrecy. The changed text replaces this with 'the degree of care they deem appropriate' and removes the obligation to prevent unauthorized use, directly conflicting with the purpose of protecting trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent', highlighting the importance of preventing unauthorized use. By removing this prevention obligation, the modified text permits actions that would be considered misappropriation under the law, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Participant acknowledges and understands that hardware, technology, source code, and software to which Participant may have access or which are disclosed to Participant as a result of his/her visit to Google may be subject to U.S. export control laws and regulations. Participant hereby certifies that he/she shall not -- directly or indirectly -- sell, export, reexport, transfer, divert, or otherwise dispose of any hardware, software, source code, or technology (including products derived from or based on such technology or software) received from Google to any country (or national thereof) without obtaining prior authorization from Google and the appropriate government authorities.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant acknowledges that hardware, technology, source code, and software to which Participant may have access or which are disclosed to Participant as a result of his/her visit to Google may be subject to U.S. export control laws and regulations. Participant's compliance with such laws is solely their discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause creates an explicit obligation to comply with export control laws. Changing it to state that compliance is at the participant's discretion directly contradicts federal export control regulations, which mandate adherence.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), specifically 15 CFR § 732.3, which outlines the steps for determining export control requirements. Making compliance discretionary violates the mandatory nature of these regulations.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original explicit obligation to comply with U.S. export control laws with a statement that compliance is solely at the participant's discretion. This directly contradicts the mandatory nature of export control regulations. The Law Explanation mentions 15 CFR § 732.3 of the Export Administration Regulations (EAR). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 only provides the title 'eCFR :: 15 CFR 732.3 -- Steps regarding the ten general prohibitions', it does highlight that this regulation exists. Furthermore, Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that the EAR navigates regulations under 15 CFR 730-774. By making compliance discretionary, the changed text undermines the legally mandated adherence to export control laws, creating a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect until such time as all Confidential Information of Google disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of Participant. Participant, upon Google's written request, will promptly return all Confidential Information received from Google, together with all copies, or certify in writing that all such Confidential Information and copies thereof have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in effect at Google's sole discretion. Participant is not obligated to return or destroy Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a clear term and obligations to return/destroy confidential information. Removing the specific term and eliminating the obligation to return/destroy the information, and setting the contract to continue solely at Google's discretion, undermines the entire purpose of the NDA from the participant's perspective.
perturbation.law_explanation: This change could contradict fundamental principles of contract law related to consideration and mutuality of obligation, reflected in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 71. By making the agreement's duration and the participant's obligations entirely dependent on Google's discretion, the agreement may lack sufficient consideration and mutuality, making it potentially unenforceable. A contract requires both parties to be bound by its terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Home | College of Law | West Virginia University Home About Us Academics Admissions Faculty and Staff Alumni & Giving Career Services Student Life Law Library Clinical Law Centers SPOTLIGHT: A collection of wvu law stories Read about some of our students, alumni and professors Top News WVU Law Student Receives “Best Overall Attorney” Recognition at National Competition "Coming Home": Professor Annie Eisenberg discusses returning to Morgantown and her favorite teaching moments Headlines WVU Law Professor Selected for Michigan Junior Scholars Conference Deem family gift establishes WVU College of Law professorship WVU Law Students Benefit from Generous WV State Bar Gift More News Events WVU Law Calendar Excel. WVU Law excels in teaching and training future lawyers in a rigorous and supportive academic setting. Our students build an essential foundation of core legal knowledge their first year, then focus on their expertise in their second and third year. As the only law school in the state, we are able to prepare lawyers by providing exceptional practice-ready learning opportunities and unparalleled access to the professional legal community. Faculty Academic Programs Scholarship Lead. We believe lawyers are leaders — in their field, in their place of employment, and in their community. Honing those skills is a key component of a WVU Law education, from taking a specialized class or being involved with a student organization. WVU Law also leads by example, addressing vital issues such as energy and sustainability, consumer matters, and diversity in the law. West Virginia Law Review Center for Energy and Sustainable Development Center for Consumer Law and Education 2020 Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion Strategic Plan Serve. Providing a service for others is the essence of the legal profession. At WVU Law, our award-winning law clinics help those in need while giving students practical experience working with clients. Other opportunities to serve include joining the Public Interest Advocates’ fellowship program or volunteering for a local organization. WVU Law also serves the ongoing educational needs of the legal community. Service is an integral part of our mission as a land-grant institution. Law Clinics Public Service Access to Justice Commission Spotlight Elaine Waterhouse Wilson Natalia Watkins Aliah Hasan: Finding Opportunity in an Unexpected Place Jacobs and Ridgeway: Peer Academic Consultants 6,000+ WVU Law alumni worldwide 51% Class of 2021 working in Private Practice 17% First-Generation College Students Who We Are WVU Law's mission is to prepare 21st century lawyers and leaders to serve the public, government, and business—both locally and globally—while focusing on justice, ethics, professionalism, and service in a diverse, vibrant, and respectful community. As the only law school in the state, we provide students with unparalleled networking opportunities and exclusive access to the legal community. Client-Ready Focus We teach the law in ways that prepare students to be client-ready the day they graduate. Our classroom experiences are enhanced by widely available experiential learning opportunities through law clinics serving those in need; externships in nonprofit, government, and judicial settings; and legal work during the summer or academic year that prepares our students for a fulfilling career in the law. Leading Edge Law Since 1878, WVU Law has provided law students with the professional education and experiences they need to succeed in an ever-changing world. Well into our second century, this includes focusing on law in public service; preparing lawyers to help shape the global energy discussion; and helping communities better manage their land use and sustainable development initiatives. perturbation.justification: The original contract requires the participant to return or destroy confidential information and specifies the agreement's duration. The changed text eliminates this obligation and allows the agreement to continue solely at Google's discretion. This contradicts the principle of mutuality of obligation and consideration. The Law Explanation specifies that this change could contradict fundamental principles of contract law related to consideration and mutuality of obligation, reflected in Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 71. While the scraped snippets are not directly applicable, the Law Explanation highlights the potential unenforceability due to the lack of consideration and mutuality, supporting the assessment of a legal contradiction. The modification removes core obligations from the participant, potentially rendering the contract unenforceable. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, because the removal of the term length and obligation creates a contract that deviates from legally recognized requirements for enforceability.
